We know that sample mean of a Bernoulli trial is unbiased and a sufﬁcient statistic for p(Z(t) = 1). Hence, ˆV(i,j) is the minimum variance unbiased estimator of p(Z(t) = 1) [2]. Theorem 3.2 stated below is about the rate of convergence of the loss calculated using empirical V to the true loss in terms of the number of target points M for n= 1. Theorem 3.2. Let ρ2(V) = 1 N2 ∑N i,j=1 liljV(i,j), whereli = (f(xi) −yi) and lj = (f(xj) −yj) then forn= 1, |ρ2(Vtrue) −ρ2( ˆV)|≤ √ log M M ∑N i,j=1 |lilj| N2 with probability≥1 − 2 M2 . Proof. For ease of notation, we prove the theorem assuming continuous µtarget(.) (check footnote1) For convenience assume mij = max(xi,xj). Further we know that Vtrue(i,j) = 1 −µtarget(mij), ˆV(i,j) = 1 M M∑ q=1 Itq≥mij = 1 − 1 M M∑ q=1 Itq<mij We deﬁne DKS ≜ supx ⏐⏐⏐⏐⏐ 1 M M∑ q=1 Itq<x −µtarget(x) ⏐⏐⏐⏐⏐ DKS is popularly known as Kolmogorov-Smirnov distance [7, 12]. Therefore, we have ⏐⏐⏐Vtrue(i,j) −ˆV(i,j) ⏐⏐⏐= ⏐⏐⏐⏐⏐ 1 M M∑ q=1 Itq<mij −µtarget(mij) ⏐⏐⏐⏐⏐ ≤DKS 1For cumulative distribution functions that are not left continuous, we can replaceµtarget(x) by µtarget(x−) at points of discontinuity. 7⏐⏐⏐ρ2(Vtrue) −ρ2( ˆV) ⏐⏐⏐= ⏐⏐⏐⏐⏐⏐ 1 N2 N∑ i,j=1 lilj [ Vtrue(i,j) −ˆV(i,j) ] ⏐⏐⏐⏐⏐⏐ ≤ 1 N2 N∑ i,j=1 |lilj| ⏐⏐⏐Vtrue(i,j) −ˆV(i,j) ⏐⏐⏐ ≤ 1 N2 DKS N∑ i,j=1 |lilj| Now using Massart’s Inequality [3], Pr(DKS >ϵ) <2e−2Mϵ2 .Therefore the statement of theorem follows by choosing ϵ= √ log M M . For more dimensions, we have the following Theorem 3.3. Theorem 3.3. Given Vtrue and ˆV, and assuming bounded|lilj|for anyiand j, the lower bound on the probability that the empirical loss function is withinδ′of the true loss function for a givenδ >0 is given byPr( ⏐⏐⏐ρ2(Vtrue) −ρ2( ˆV) ⏐⏐⏐≤δ′) ≥1 −N(N + 1)e−2Mδ2 ,where δ′ = δmaxi,j|lilj|. This suggests thatM >clog(N) δ2 with c> 1 leads to convergence in probability for anyδ >0. Proof. We ﬁrst note that ⏐⏐⏐ρ2(Vtrue) −ρ2( ˆV) ⏐⏐⏐≤ 1 N2 ∑ i,j |lilj| ⏐⏐⏐Vtrue(i,j) −ˆV(i,j) ⏐⏐⏐. (12) We prove this theorem by giving a bound for 1 N2 ∑ i,j ⏐⏐⏐Vtrue(i,j) −ˆV(i,j) ⏐⏐⏐. From the proof of Lemma 3.1, ˆV(i,j) denotes the sample mean of i.i.d. Bernoulli random variables with Vtrue(i,j) being the expected value. Hence, by the Hoeffding inequality Pr( ⏐⏐⏐Vtrue(i,j) −ˆV(i,j) ⏐⏐⏐>δ) <2e−2Mδ2 Now, we can use the union bound and the fact that |Vtrue(i,j) −ˆV(i,j)|= |Vtrue(j,i) −ˆV(j,i)| to get Pr( 1 N2 ∑ i,j ⏐⏐⏐Vtrue(i,j) −ˆV(i,j) ⏐⏐⏐≤δ) ≥1 −2(N(N −1) 2 + N)e−2Mδ2 = 1 −N(N + 1)e−2Mδ2 Now letting δ′= δmaxi,j|lilj|, we use inequality (12) to obtain the required result. 3.2 Algorithm Our algorithm uses a sample from the target distribution, T to calculate the V-matrix. The worst case complexity of calculating the V-matrix is O(N2M). The full complexity of learning will depend on the choice of learning algorithm. We describe these algorithms for the V-matrix proposed in Eq. (11) for the V-SVM learning algorithm. This framework gives a loss function in Eq. (7), so it can be easily adapted to different choices of learning models like decision tree based gradient boosting [4]. See the appendix for additional details on using the V-matrix framework in the context of gradient boosting. Algorithmic Remark:In higher dimensions the ˆV-matrix stated in Eq. 11 can be ill-conditioned. [17] suggests using an additive version of the V-matrix. The derivation this version considers the em- pirical CDF for each dimension separately, minimizing the sum of theL2 errors (for details refer Equa- tion 55 in [17]). Thus, Eq. 11 instead becomes ˆVadditive(i,j) = 1 nM ∑M q=1 ∑n k=1 Itkq ≥max(xk i ,xk j ). This is described in Algorithm 2. We use ˆVadditive for some of our experiments on real datasets. 8(a)  (b)  (c) Figure 5: (a) Robustness of methods with 200 training points. (b) Normalized L2-error in Experiment 2. (c) Normalized L2-error in Experiment 2 using a different target sample. 3.3 Experiments on Synthetic Datasets We conducted multiple experiments to show the applicability and effectiveness of the proposed ˆV-matrix given by Eq. (11). In both the experiments below, a gaussian KDE with bandwidth h = 2 .0 is chosen for [ 11, 14, 19]. Further τ = 0 .5 for [14]. For Huang et al. [ 5] method, B = 1000,ϵ = √ N−1√ N ,σ = 0.1,where N is the size of training set. KLIEP and uLSIF are evaluated using the matlab code on the authors’ website [15, 6]. The training data comprises {(xi,yi)}N i=1, where xi ∈[−1,1] and yi ∈{0,1}such that p(yi = 1|xi) = 1 1+e5xi .The training data {xi}N i=1 is generated using distribution p(x) ∼Uniform[−1,1] and the test data {tq}M q=1 is generated using distribution q(t) ∼Uniform[0,1] with probability 0.3 and Uniform[−1,0] with probability 0.7. In Experiments 1 and 2 below, a SVM is used with a square rooted Gaussian kernel with kernel width = 1 and regularization coefﬁcient γ = 0.1. Experiment 1: Robustness Figure 5a shows the stability of the predicted p(y = 1|x) for different methods on a single run. N = 200 training points and M = 1000 testing points were used. We demonstrate that, like theV-matrix derived in [17] (given in Example 2.2), the proposed ˆV-matrix in Equation 11 is stable in its prediction of conditional probability. In contrast, the predictions by unweighted L2-loss (V = I) and the weighted L2-loss using Shimodaira et al. [11], Huang et al. [5], Sugiyama et al. [14, 15], and Kanamori et al. [6] are less smooth, indicating instability. Experiment 2: Probability Prediction Error Figure 5b compares the normalized L2-error of the predicted conditional probability function for various methods. The V-matrix framework is designed to minimize this error, so the improvement is not surprising. Our empirical V-matrix further improves this error because it takes into account the differences between the training and target distributions. Figure 5c instead calculates this V-matrix on a different set of 500 samples from the test distribution. Both Figures 5b and 5c are averaged over 50 trials. Remark: The error curves for Sugiyama et al. [ 14], KLIEP [15], uLSIF [6] and Shimodaira et al. [11] methods in Figures 5b and 5c are overlapping as the mean errors are close. 3.4 Experiments on Real Datasets These experiments use typical datasets from previous papers [ 5, 6] and synthetically generated covariate shift, as is standard in [5, 14, 15, 6]. [11, 14] represent different regularizations of the ratio of KDE densities and perform similarly, so we only show [11]. Further, the performance obtained for uLSIF [6] and RuLSIF [19] are similar, so we have only included uLSIF. Results given by [5] may not be optimally tuned, as ﬁnding the best values for hyper-parameters is difﬁcult. In all the experiments below, we normalize all feature vectors to be in [0,1]n (nis the dimension of feature vector). 9Experiment 3: Replicating [6] In this experiment, we bias the datasets by following the method used in [6]. We randomly choose c from 1 to nand ﬁx it for each trial. A sample xk is chosen randomly from the dataset and is accepted in the test (target) set with probability min(1,4(xc k)2).The sample xk is removed from the dataset whether or not it was accepted into the test set. We continue until we have 500 test samples. A training set of size 100 is chosen uniformly at random from the remaining samples in the dataset. The mean performance error over 100 trials for twonorm and ringnorm datasets [9] is provided in Table 1. For the ringnorm dataset, 5 features were randomly chosen in each trial for classiﬁcation. We used additive version of the V-matrix when testing our method. In Table 1, we observe a consistent good performance by our method for both the datasets, outperforming other methods on twonorm data. KLIEP and uLSIF do not improve on the twonorm data but perform the best on the ringnorm data. The cancer, diabetes and banknote datasets did not have enough samples to generate 500 test points with the biasing technique described here, so we restricted this experiment to the datasets used in [6]. Experiment 4: Single Feature Bias This experiment is a generalization of the one used in [ 5]. 100 training points are biased by ﬁrst randomly selecting a single feature. We then decide to bias the feature up or down down. If biasing up, we increase the probability of selecting a datapoint with a feature value larger than the median by a factor of 4. If biasing down, we decrease its probability by a factor of 4. Each dataset biasing was performed 100 times. Here we use the additive version of the empirical V-matrix. This biasing scheme is challenging for the previous methods – none are able to achieve signiﬁcant improvement over the unweighted classiﬁer for the cancer, diabetes, banknote, and ringnorm datasets. Our method improves performance in the banknote and ringnorm datasets. Further, our method is stable in that it never signiﬁcantly exceeds the error of an unweighted classiﬁer (see cancer and twonorm in Table 1). [6] occasionally performs signiﬁcantly worse than an unweighted classiﬁer. Experiment 5: Multiple Feature Bias This experiment is similar to Experiment 4, except that it biases the L2 norm of the feature vector instead of a speciﬁc feature. The biasing scheme for the norm is the same as given for a single feature in Experiment 4. That is, we bias the selection of datapoints with larger than median L2 norm up or down by a factor of 4. Because there is less variation between the datasets generated by this biasing method, the mean performance error is given over 50 trials. As with Experiment 4, some datasets paired with this biasing scheme are too difﬁcult for any of the methods tested. Our method is the only method to improve signiﬁcantly in the banknote and ringnorm datasets. The twonorm dataset is the only case where our method underperforms an unweighted classiﬁer, and it does so with a large variance. The number of input dimensions in each dataset is given in parentheses. 104 Conclusion Our method makes two important contributions towards more effective ways to handle covariate shift. First, it removes the need to tune parameters like kernel bandwidth by switching to a CDF-based framework. Second, in addition to having comparable or superior performance in classiﬁcation tasks, our framework is signiﬁcantly more robust in its predictions of conditional probability of labels. Though we focused on the pairwise classiﬁcation problem in this paper, our covariate shift method can also be extended to multiclassiﬁcation by using one-hot encoding for labels and to regression by using the regression version of the Fredholm integral equation (see details in [17] for these settings). Further, the V-matrix framework is not limited to SVMs and can be applied to other machine learning algorithms using the loss function described in Eq. (7). For instance, we derive the closed form solution for the gradient boosting algorithm [4], and give this loss function in the appendix. These loss functions can be minimized by standard gradient descent techniques. A limitation is our method’s inability to handle large dimensional data. While using the additive version of the V-matrix provides a ﬁx, future work may explore forms of dimension reduction that allow the use of the original multiplicative V-matrix, which may further improve performance. Broader Impact Machine learning is limited by the availability and quality of data. In many circumstances we may not have access to labeled data from our target distribution. Improving methods for covariate shift will help us extend the impact of the data we do have. Stably predicting a conditional probability distribution has an application that has recently gained notorious prominence. In the presence of a pandemic, one may wish to predict the death-rate to calculate the expected toll on society. This translates exactly to predicting the conditional probability of dying given demographic information. Data that has been gathered often has a large sampling bias, since a persons risk proﬁle may affect their willingness to leave home and participate in a study, and their age may affect their availability for an online survey. A stable method for covariate shift in this situation can be a critical part of ensuring ofﬁcials have accurate statistics when making decisions. One potential negative impact of the work would be if it were to be misunderstood and misused, yielding incorrect results. This is a concern in all areas of data science, so it is important that the conditions for appropriate use be well understood. Appendix Gradient Boosting withV matrix In the gradient boosting framework introduced by Friedman [4], the estimate ˆyiof yiis mathematically modeled as follows: ˆyi = K∑ k=1 fk(xi), f k ∈F (13) where Kis the number of trees, f is a function the functional space Fand Fis the set all possible Classiﬁcation and Regression Trees (CARTs). The objective function to be optimized is given by obj = n∑ i=1 l(yi,ˆyi) + K∑ k=1 Ω(fk) (14) However, in this loss function the samples xi are assumed to be independent. In a realistic scenario, samples may not be independent, for example, the samples may be derived from different races and 11genders giving rise to implicit bias in the training dataset. To encounter this, we can have a loss function that takes into account the associations between different loss function. This is tackled by using a V-matrix which also measures the distance between the sample points. Formally, the objective or loss function is given as follows: obj = n∑ i=1 n∑ j=1 l(yi,yj,ˆyi,ˆyj,Vij) + K∑ k=1 Ω(fk) (15) Note we use Vij to denote V(i,j) here. The proof below deals with a special case when l(yi,yj,ˆyi,ˆyj,Vij) = (yi −ˆyi)(yj −ˆyj)Vij. llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Introduces a parameter-free, CDF-based method to correct covariate shift by replacing density-ratio estimation with an empirical cumulative distribution function (ECDF) approach. Generalizes Vapnik & Izmailov’s V-matrix framework to non-uniform target distributions by computing the V-matrix from unlabeled target samples. Proves the empirical V-matrix is the MVUE of the true V (Lemma 3.1) and provides convergence rates for 1D (Theorem 3.2) and multi-dimensional settings (Theorem 3.3). Demonstrates improved robustness of conditional probability estimates and comparable or better classification performance to KDE/importance-weighting and direct-ratio baselines on synthetic and real datasets, without parameter tuning.
llm_extracted_info.methodology:
- Problem setting: Covariate shift with p(x) != q(x) and p(y|x) = q(y|x); access to unlabeled samples from target q(x).
- Fredholm framework: Estimate f(x)=p(y=1|x) via minimizing L(f)=||F1-F2||_2^2 + γW(f), where F1(x)=∫θ(x−x′)f(x′)dP(x′) and F2(x)=P(y=1,x). The empirical loss reduces to a quadratic form using a V-matrix: ρ^2 = (1/N^2) ∑_{i,j} (f(x_i)-y_i)(f(x_j)-y_j) V(i,j).
- Empirical V-matrix from target ECDF: Given target samples {t_q}_{q=1}^M, define ˆV(i,j) = (1/M) ∑_q ∏_{k=1}^n I[t_q^{(k)} ≥ max(x_i^{(k)}, x_j^{(k)})] (for φ=1). For high dimensions, use an additive version: ˆV_add(i,j) = (1/(nM)) ∑_q ∑_{k=1}^n I[t_q^{(k)} ≥ max(x_i^{(k)}, x_j^{(k)})]. Complexity to compute V is O(N^2 M).
- Learning model: V-SVM with kernel K and regularization γ; closed-form solution for coefficients A and bias c using (VK+γI)^{-1}V. Also outlines how to plug the V-matrix into gradient boosting via a pairwise loss.
- Theory: ˆV is MVUE of V_true (Lemma 3.1). 1D convergence via KS-distance and Massart’s inequality: |ρ^2(V_true)−ρ^2(ˆV)| ≤ sqrt(log M / M) * (∑_{i,j} |l_i l_j|)/N^2 with prob ≥ 1−2/M^2. Multidim Hoeffding+union bound: Pr(|ρ^2(V_true)−ρ^2(ˆV)| ≤ δ′) ≥ 1 − N(N+1)e^{−2Mδ^2}, with δ′ = δ max_{i,j}|l_i l_j|, implying M = Ω((log N)/δ^2).
- Key properties: requires no density estimation or bandwidth/regularization tuning; uses only unlabeled target samples to shape the loss via V.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup:
- Synthetic data:
  - Conditional: p(y=1|x)=1/(1+e^{5x}).
  - Training covariates: p(x) ~ Uniform[-1,1]. Target covariates: mixture q(x) ~ 0.3·Uniform[0,1] + 0.7·Uniform[-1,0].
  - Models: V-SVM with square-rooted Gaussian kernel (width=1), γ=0.1.
  - Baselines: Importance weighting via KDE ratio [11] (Gaussian KDE bandwidth h=2.0), regularized ratio [14] with τ=0.5, RuLSIF [19] (via KDE), Huang et al. moment-matching [5] (B=1000, ε=√(N−1)/√N, σ=0.1), KLIEP [15], uLSIF [6] (authors’ code).
  - Evaluations: (i) Robustness/shape of p̂(y=1|x) (N=200, M=1000). (ii) L2 error of probability estimates averaged over 50 trials; also tested with a different target sample set (M=500).
- Real datasets (features normalized to [0,1]^n): twonorm, ringnorm [9]; breast cancer (Wisconsin) [18]; diabetes (Pima) [13]; banknote authentication [21].
  - Experiment 3 (replicating [6]): Construct target set (size 500) by accepting samples with prob min(1,4(x_c)^2) for randomly chosen feature c; training set size 100. 100 trials. For ringnorm, 5 features randomly chosen per trial. Used additive V.
  - Experiment 4 (single feature bias): For 100 training points, randomly select a feature, bias selection above/below median by factor 4 (up or down). 100 trials. Used additive V.
  - Experiment 5 (multiple feature bias): Bias based on L2 norm above/below median by factor 4; 50 trials. Used additive V.
  - Metrics: Classification error normalized to unweighted classifier (1.0 = equal performance), reporting mean (std). Findings: proposed method consistently competitive, often improves (notably on banknote and ringnorm), with stable performance and rare large degradations versus baselines that are sensitive to tuning and can underperform unweighted.
llm_extracted_info.limitations:
- High-dimensional settings can make the multiplicative V ill-conditioned; relies on an additive V approximation that may lose inter-dimensional interactions.
- Requires access to unlabeled target samples; performance depends on sufficient M for accurate ECDF.
- Computational cost to form V is O(N^2 M), which can be heavy for large N and M.
- While robust for probability estimation, classification gains vary by dataset; in some cases (e.g., twonorm under certain biases) it may underperform or show higher variance.
- Theoretical bounds for multidimensional convergence are conservative; no tight rates beyond Hoeffding/union bound are provided.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions:
- Develop principled dimension reduction to enable use of the original multiplicative V in high dimensions and improve conditioning.
- Extend and validate the approach for multiclass classification and regression tasks (suggested by the Fredholm framework), including large-scale implementations.
- Algorithmic improvements for scalable V computation (e.g., sampling, blocking, low-rank approximations, coresets) and online/streaming variants.
- Explore integration with additional learners (e.g., deep networks) via V-induced losses and study optimization dynamics.
- Derive sharper finite-sample guarantees in higher dimensions and under dependence/structure in features.
- Investigate semi-supervised or weakly supervised settings where limited labeled target data is available to further refine V or f(x).
- Study robustness under different forms of dataset shift (beyond covariate shift), and fairness-related applications where stable probability estimates are crucial.

new_method.method:
Motivation
- Distribution-mismatch in KD is context-conditional: the teacher’s confidence/margin systematically varies with input contexts (augmentation states, photometric/style factors, scale/translation/blur). Students inherit these biases, hurting cross-architecture transfer, long-tail × rare-context cells, calibration, and worst-group robustness under strong augmentation and OOD.
- Gaps: (i) Student-driven reweighting (IPWD/WSL) is unstable early and agnostic to context balance; (ii) temperature schemes (TTM/WTTM) do not modulate by context; (iii) CDF/V-matrix shows the power of ECDFs for shift without density ratios, but has not been used to smooth teacher “context × margin” in KD; (iv) worst-group risk is rarely optimized in KD despite clear context-skew.
- Need: A teacher-statistics–only, early-stable, plug-in mechanism that equalizes per-context boundary-distance distributions, controls worst-group risk, and adapts teacher sharpness so students do not inherit context bias. 

Methodology
High-level: Build per-context ECDFs of the teacher’s boundary-distance proxy and use quantiles to drive three coupled controls: (A) intra-context weights, (B) inter-context DRO group weights, and (C) context-adaptive teacher temperature. Optionally normalize the teacher’s logits per context via a lightweight quantile-equalization.

1) Context discovery (no labels required)
- Known contexts from augmentation logs: discretize brightness/contrast/saturation, crop scale/aspect, translation, blur strength into K bins; concatenate bin IDs.
- Unsupervised style contexts: extract CLIP/DINO features → PCA → online k-means (EMA); cluster IDs represent style contexts.
- Hybrid context ID: concat(aug-bin || style-cluster), with hierarchical backoff for sparse cells (merge nearest style clusters by cosine similarity).

2) Teacher-only ECDFs and rank-stable intra-context weighting
- Boundary-distance proxy m(x,t): by default top-1 minus top-2 logit gap; alternatives 1−entropy or correct-minus-runner-up if labels exist.
- Maintain online quantile sketches (e.g., t-digest) per context t and globally: F_t(m), F_all(m). For sample x in context t, compute quantiles q_t=F_t(m(x,t)) and q_ref=F_all(m(x)).
- Intra-context weight (rank-invariant): w_ctx(x,t) = ((q_ref+δ)/(q_t+δ))^β, clipped to [cmin,cmax]. Intuition: for the same absolute difficulty, tail-side points in a biased context get upweighted to equalize gradient mass across contexts early and stably (no student signals used).

3) Inter-context DRO for worst-group control
- Track per-context moving-average loss Ĺ_t and update group weights via exponentiated gradient: α_t ∝ exp(λ(Ĺ_t − Ĺ̄)); normalize and clip. This realizes a distributionally robust reweighting over discovered contexts, explicitly optimizing worst-group accuracy.

4) Context-adaptive teacher temperature
- Set temperature by context-quantile: T_t(x) = T_min + (T_max − T_min)·(1 − q_t)^γ. Low-quantile (hard tail) points get higher T, reducing overconfidence and preventing transfer of teacher’s context skew; the student temperature stays fixed (TTM-compatible).

5) Optional: Quantile-Equalized Teacher (QET)
- Learn per-context affine transform on teacher logits y→s_t·y+b_t to match the per-context margin ECDF to the global reference in Wasserstein-1 on a small FIFO buffer. Update s_t, b_t with strong regularization; s_t≈1 for well-matched contexts. This neutralizes teacher context bias at the source without modifying the student loss form.

6) Final loss and schedule (drop-in to KD codebases)
- Loss per batch: L = L_ce + α · Σ_t α_t · E_{x∈t}[ w_ctx(x,t) · KL(p_t^{T_t(x)} || p_s) ].
- Two-stage stability: Warm-up epochs only collect ECDFs (w_ctx=1, α_t uniform, T_t=T_base). Then ramp β, λ, and (T_max−T_min) with cosine schedule; enable QET after ECDFs stabilize. Optionally in late fine-tune, multiply by small student-dependent weights (IPWD^η · WSL^{1−η}, η≈0.2) for extra polish without destabilizing early training.

7) Reliability, efficiency, and implementation
- Rare-context smoothing: Beta(0.5,0.5) pseudo-counts for quantiles; hierarchical blending across nearest contexts by CLIP-space similarity when cell counts are small.
- Safety: clip w_ctx and α_t (e.g., [0.25,4]), gradient-norm clip, T in [2,8].
- Overhead: one t-digest per context (KBs), O(log N) queries, tens of lines added around the KD loss. No extra student heads.
- Integration: a ContextManager yields context_id; ECDFStore maps id→t-digest; compute q_t,q_ref→w_ctx, α_t via EG, T_t(x); optionally apply QET to teacher logits; replace KD loss with the weighted, DRO-aggregated, context-temperature KD. CE untouched. Zero test-time overhead.

8) How CCAD differs from prior work
- Versus IPWD/WSL/TTM: CCAD is teacher-only and rank-based (ECDF), enabling early stability and explicit context balancing; adds DRO to optimize worst-group risk and sets temperature by context quantile rather than globally or by student signals.
- Versus CDF/V-matrix: we use ECDFs internally to KD to equalize teacher margin distributions across contexts—no density ratios/propensity estimation; adds context-adaptive temperature and logit-side quantile normalization.
- Versus AFD/feature KD: orthogonal; CCAD operates at logits with negligible overhead and can pair with feature losses.
- Versus KD3A/BETA: not a DA/pseudo-labeling framework; no source/target separation or multi-source consensus—purely single-teacher KD with on-the-fly context balancing.

Evaluation plan (concise)
- Benchmarks: CIFAR-100, ImageNet-1K; robustness on ImageNet-C/A, Stylized-ImageNet; long-tail splits; strong RandAug/RandAugMix. Pairs include cross-architecture (e.g., ViT→ResNet, ResNet→MobileNet).
- Metrics: Top-1, ECE, worst-group accuracy over discovered contexts, tail-quantile accuracy, OOD performance, calibration under shift. Ablations on each component (w_ctx, α_t, T_t, QET) and sensitivity to K, β, λ, γ.

Expected impact
- Robust, early-stable KD that mitigates teacher context bias without student dependence, improving cross-architecture transfer, worst-group accuracy, and calibration under augmentation and domain shift.
- Strong gains for rare context × class cells via quantile-based equalization and explicit DRO—addressing distribution mismatch at the granularity that causes it.
- Practicality: trivial to adopt in existing KD stacks, minimal overhead, no extra data or discriminators; extends naturally to detection/segmentation (region-level contexts) and multimodal KD (audio/text environmental embeddings).

experimental_design.experiment_strategy:
Below are three complementary, feasible experiments that validate the method along accuracy, worst-group robustness, calibration, cross-architecture transfer, and early-training stability. Each plan includes concrete setup, baselines, metrics, ablations, and Python-implementable steps.

Experiment 1: CIFAR-100 Cross-Architecture KD with Discovered Contexts
- Goal
  - Show that context-conditioned ECDF equalization improves average accuracy, worst-group accuracy across discovered contexts, tail-quantile accuracy, and calibration, while remaining early-training stable.
- Setup
  - Dataset: CIFAR-100 with strong augmentation (RandAugment or RandAugMix + random crop/flip/color jitter/blur). Train split only; test on clean CIFAR-100.
  - Teacher: WideResNet-70-16 or ViT-S/16 (pretrained or trained to >80% top-1).
  - Student: ResNet-18 or MobileNetV2.
  - Contexts (hybrid):
    - Aug bins: brightness, contrast, crop scale (3 bins each) => 27 aug-contexts.
    - Style clusters: Precompute DINOv2-S or CLIP ViT-B/16 features on training images; PCA(64) then k-means k=8.
    - Hybrid ID: concat(aug-bin || style-cluster). Expected cells: 27*8=216; enable hierarchical backoff by merging to nearest style cluster when count<50.
  - Boundary-distance proxy m(x): teacher top1 - top2 logit margin.
- Training components
  - ECDF store: t-digest (pip install tdigest) per context and a global t-digest; update during warm-up.
  - Intra-context weight: w_ctx = ((q_ref+δ)/(q_t+δ))^β, clipped [0.25, 4].
  - Inter-context DRO: α_t via exponentiated gradient on per-context moving-average KD loss Ĺ_t.
  - Context-adaptive temperature: T_t(x) = T_min + (T_max − T_min)*(1 − q_t)^γ, clipped [2,8].
  - Optional QET: per-context affine logits y→s_t*y + b_t, learned with small FIFO buffer and W1 loss between context and global margin distributions (strong L2 reg on s_t,b_t).
  - Loss: L = CE + α * Σ_t α_t * E_{x∈t}[ w_ctx * KL(p_t^{T_t} || p_s) ].
  - Schedule: warm-up 10 epochs (collect ECDF, set w=1, α_t uniform, T=T_base=4); ramp β from 0→1.5, λ from 0→2.0, T_max from 4→7 with cosine over epochs 11–200; enable QET at epoch 25.
- Baselines
  - Vanilla KD (Hinton) with fixed T=4.
  - TTM/WTTM (temperature tuning schemes) with global T schedule.
  - IPWD and WSL (student-driven reweighting) with recommended settings.
  - CCAD ablations: -w_ctx only; -α_t only; -T_t only; +QET; full CCAD.
- Metrics
  - Top-1 on clean test.
  - ECE (15-bin) and NLL on clean.
  - Worst-group accuracy across discovered hybrid contexts (min over groups with ≥100 test samples).
  - Tail-quantile accuracy: accuracy on samples in q_t ≤ 0.1 during training (logged by matching contexts on test without updates).
  - Early stability: variance of batch KD loss in epochs 1–20; gradient norm and its CV; fraction of exploding gradients.
  - Efficiency: wall-clock overhead vs vanilla KD.
- Expected outcome
  - CCAD improves worst-group accuracy and tail-quantile accuracy, reduces ECE, and shows smoother early loss with fewer spikes than IPWD/WSL.
- Implementation notes
  - Use tdigest.TDigest per context; maintain EMA losses per context for DRO.
  - Clip α_t ∈ [0.25, 4] and renormalize.
  - If tdigest unavailable, use per-context fixed-size reservoir buffers (e.g., 512) and approximate quantiles with torch.quantile.

Experiment 2: ImageNet-100 Robustness and OOD Shift (ImageNet-C and Stylized-ImageNet)
- Goal
  - Validate robustness and calibration under distribution shift and worst-group control; demonstrate cross-architecture transfer.
- Setup
  - Dataset: ImageNet-100 (100-class subset) for tractability. Evaluate on clean val, ImageNet-C (all types, severities 1–5), Stylized-ImageNet (val split).
  - Teacher: ViT-B/16 (pretrained on ImageNet-1K).
  - Students: ResNet-50 and MobileNetV3-Large.
  - Contexts
    - Aug bins: brightness, contrast, saturation, crop scale (3 bins each) => 81.
    - Style clusters: CLIP ViT-B/16 features on train split, PCA(128), k=16.
    - Hybrid: 81*16=1296; enable backoff merging by nearest centroids; maintain at most 400 active contexts via frequency thresholding.
  - Apply strong RandAugment during training; log augmentation parameters for context IDs.
- Training settings
  - Same CCAD components as Experiment 1; warm-up 5 epochs (shorter dataset), train 120 epochs; β_max=1.0, λ_max=1.5, T_min=2, T_max=8, γ=1.2.
  - QET enabled after epoch 10 with strong regularization (λ_qet=1e-3 on s_t,b_t).
- Baselines
  - Vanilla KD, TTM/WTTM, IPWD, WSL; plus a GroupDRO baseline that uses discovered hybrid contexts but without ECDF weighting or adaptive T (to isolate the effect of CCAD’s rank-based reweighting).
- Metrics
  - Clean: Top-1/Top-5, ECE, NLL.
  - Corruption: mCE (mean Corruption Error), mFR (flip rate between severities), ECE-C (ECE on corrupted sets), AUROC for confidence vs error on corrupted data.
  - Worst-group acc: min over hybrid contexts on clean and on a union over top-5 frequent corruption types.
  - Calibration shift: ECE gap between clean and severity-5 average.
  - Cross-arch transfer: report deltas for ViT→ResNet-50 vs ViT→MobileNetV3.
- Expected outcome
  - CCAD lowers mCE and ECE-C vs baselines, narrows calibration shift, improves worst-group acc (especially on low-contrast/strong-blur contexts), and benefits the smaller MobileNet more.
- Feasibility
  - With mixed precision and 2×A100/3090, 120 epochs on IN-100 with strong aug is practical. If compute-limited, train 60 epochs and subsample ImageNet-C severities.

Experiment 3: Synthetic 2D Context-Bias Toy to Probe Mechanism and Early Stability
- Goal
  - Isolate the mechanism: show ECDF-based w_ctx and adaptive T_t correct a teacher with context-dependent margin bias, improving worst-group acc and calibration without using student signals.
- Setup
  - Data: 3-class 2D Gaussians with 4 contexts c ∈ {A,B,C,D}. Each context has identical class means/covariances but undergoes a context-specific margin bias in the teacher: we simulate the teacher by training a well-fit MLP and then modifying its logits with a context-specific scale and bias so that margin distributions differ between contexts (e.g., s_c ∈ {0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0}, b_c ∈ {−0.3, 0, 0.2, 0.4}).
  - Student: 2-layer MLP.
  - Contexts: known (the synthetic c), no style clusters needed.
- Procedure
  - Generate 80k train, 20k test points; 20k per context to balance true data distribution. Teacher predictions and margins computed with the biased logits.
  - Train student with KD:
    - Vanilla KD baseline.
    - CCAD (-w_ctx only), (-T_t only), full CCAD.
    - IPWD/WSL baselines to compare early instability.
  - No CE on labels to stress KD; optional additional runs with CE included.
- Metrics
  - Overall and per-context accuracy; worst-group accuracy.
  - ECE and NLL (calibration); reliability diagrams per context.
  - Early stability: batch KD loss variance and gradient norm CV in first 5 epochs.
  - Distribution alignment check: per-context ECDFs of student margin vs teacher margin before/after CCAD to visualize equalization.
- Expected outcome
  - CCAD increases worst-group acc (esp. low-scale contexts), reduces ECE, and shows smoother early dynamics; w_ctx primarily fixes group imbalance, T_t improves calibration; combined best.
- Feasibility
  - Single-GPU or CPU; runs in minutes; ideal for rapid ablation.

Minimal PyTorch implementation skeleton for CCAD components (drop-in to KD loop)
- Dependencies
  - pip install tdigest einops

- Core utilities
  class ECDFStore:
      def __init__(self, max_contexts=1024):
          from tdigest import TDigest
          self.global_digest = TDigest()
          self.ctx = {}
          self.counts = {}
          self.max_contexts = max_contexts
      def update(self, ctx_id, margin_values):
          # margin_values: 1D torch or list
          mv = margin_values.detach().cpu().float().tolist()
          self.global_digest.batch_update(mv)
          if ctx_id not in self.ctx and len(self.ctx) >= self.max_contexts:
              # simple eviction: drop least frequent context
              drop = min(self.counts, key=self.counts.get)
              self.ctx.pop(drop, None); self.counts.pop(drop, None)
          if ctx_id not in self.ctx:
              from tdigest import TDigest
              self.ctx[ctx_id] = TDigest()
              self.counts[ctx_id] = 0
          self.ctx[ctx_id].batch_update(mv)
          self.counts[ctx_id] += len(mv)
      def quantiles(self, ctx_id, m_vals, eps=1e-4):
          # returns q_t, q_ref arrays in [0,1]
          mv = m_vals.detach().cpu().float().tolist()
          q_ref = [self.global_digest.cdf(v) for v in mv]
          if ctx_id in self.ctx and self.counts[ctx_id] > 32:  # min count
              q_t = [self.ctx[ctx_id].cdf(v) for v in mv]
          else:
              q_t = q_ref  # backoff
          import numpy as np
          q_t = np.clip(q_t, eps, 1-eps)
          q_ref = np.clip(q_ref, eps, 1-eps)
          return torch.tensor(q_t, device=m_vals.device), torch.tensor(q_ref, device=m_vals.device)

  class DROWeights:
      def __init__(self, eta=0.1, clip=(0.25,4.0)):
          self.loss_ma = {}  # EMA per context
          self.alpha = {}    # weights per context (unnormalized log-space)
          self.eta = eta
          self.clip = clip
      def update(self, ctx_ids, losses, momentum=0.9):
          # ctx_ids: list of ctx per sample; losses: per-sample KD loss
          import collections
          by_ctx = collections.defaultdict(list)
          for c,l in zip(ctx_ids, losses.detach().cpu().tolist()):
              by_ctx[c].append(l)
          # update EMA loss
          for c, ls in by_ctx.items():
              avg = sum(ls)/len(ls)
              if c not in self.loss_ma:
                  self.loss_ma[c] = avg
                  self.alpha[c] = 0.0
              else:
                  self.loss_ma[c] = momentum*self.loss_ma[c] + (1-momentum)*avg
          # compute centered losses and EG update in log-space
          if len(self.loss_ma)==0: return
          mean_loss = sum(self.loss_ma.values())/len(self.loss_ma)
          for c in self.loss_ma:
              self.alpha[c] += self.eta*(self.loss_ma[c]-mean_loss)
          # normalize and clip
          import numpy as np
          keys = list(self.alpha.keys())
          a = np.array([self.alpha[k] for k in keys])
          w = np.exp(a - a.max())
          w = w / w.sum()
          # clip
          w = np.clip(w, self.clip[0], self.clip[1])
          w = w / w.sum()
          self._cached_norm = {k: float(w[i]) for i,k in enumerate(keys)}
      def get(self, ctx_ids):
          # returns per-sample group weights alpha_t
          return torch.tensor([self._cached_norm.get(c, 1.0) for c in ctx_ids], dtype=torch.float32)

  class QET:
      def __init__(self, reg=1e-3):
          self.s = {}; self.b = {}; self.reg = reg
      def transform(self, ctx_id, logits):
          s = self.s.get(ctx_id, 1.0); b = self.b.get(ctx_id, 0.0)
          return logits * s + b
      def update(self, ctx_id, margins_ctx, margins_global, lr=1e-3):
          # simple affine fit on margins via L2 proxy for W1; keep tiny updates
          import numpy as np
          mc = margins_ctx.detach().cpu().numpy(); mg = margins_global.detach().cpu().numpy()
          if len(mc) < 16: return
          s = self.s.get(ctx_id, 1.0); b = self.b.get(ctx_id, 0.0)
          # one GD step on || s*mc + b - mg ||^2 + reg*(s-1)^2 + reg*b^2
          # approximate by matching means and variances
          mc_mean, mg_mean = mc.mean(), mg.mean()
          mc_std = mc.std() + 1e-6
          target_s = (mg.std()+1e-6)/mc_std
          target_b = mg_mean - target_s*mc_mean
          s = 0.99*s + 0.01*target_s
          b = 0.99*b + 0.01*target_b
          # regularize toward s=1,b=0
          s = s - 0.01*self.reg*(s-1)
          b = b - 0.01*self.reg*(b)
          self.s[ctx_id], self.b[ctx_id] = float(s), float(b)

- KD loop integration (pseudo-code)
  def kd_train_one_epoch(model_s, teacher, loader, ctx_manager, ecdfs, dro, qet=None,
                         alpha=1.0, beta=1.5, lam=1.5, T_min=2.0, T_max=8.0, gamma=1.2,
                         warmup=False, device='cuda'):
      model_s.train(); teacher.eval()
      import torch.nn.functional as F
      for imgs, labels, aug_meta in loader:
          imgs, labels = imgs.to(device), labels.to(device)
          # 1) context ids
          ctx_ids = ctx_manager.batch_context_ids(aug_meta, imgs)  # list[str or int]
          # 2) teacher logits and margins
          with torch.no_grad():
              logits_t = teacher(imgs)
              # optional QET at inference time
              if qet is not None and not warmup:
                  logits_t = torch.stack([qet.transform(c, lt) for c, lt in zip(ctx_ids, logits_t)])
              top2 = torch.topk(logits_t, k=2, dim=-1).values
              margins = top2[:,0] - top2[:,1]
          # 3) update ECDFs during warm-up
          if warmup:
              # per-context updates
              import collections
              by_ctx = collections.defaultdict(list)
              for c, m in zip(ctx_ids, margins):
                  by_ctx[c].append(m)
              for c, ms in by_ctx.items():
                  ecdfs.update(c, torch.stack(ms))
          # 4) quantiles and weights
          q_t_list, q_ref_list = [], []
          for c, m in zip(ctx_ids, margins):
              q_t, q_ref = ecdfs.quantiles(c, m.unsqueeze(0))
              q_t_list.append(q_t.item()); q_ref_list.append(q_ref.item())
          q_t = torch.tensor(q_t_list, device=device)
          q_ref = torch.tensor(q_ref_list, device=device)
          delta = 1e-3
          if warmup:
              w_ctx = torch.ones_like(q_t)
              T = torch.full_like(q_t, 4.0)
              alpha_t = torch.ones_like(q_t)
          else:
              w_ctx = torch.clamp(((q_ref+delta)/(q_t+delta))**beta, 0.25, 4.0)
              T = T_min + (T_max - T_min) * (1.0 - q_t).pow(gamma)
              alpha_t = dro.get(ctx_ids).to(device)
          # 5) student forward
          logits_s = model_s(imgs)
          # 6) KD loss with per-sample T
          def soft_targets(logits, T):
              return F.log_softmax(logits / T.unsqueeze(-1), dim=-1), F.softmax(logits / T.unsqueeze(-1), dim=-1)
          log_p_t, p_t = soft_targets(logits_t, T)
          log_p_s = F.log_softmax(logits_s, dim=-1)
          kd_per_sample = torch.sum(p_t * (log_p_t - log_p_s), dim=-1)
          # 7) CE loss
          ce = F.cross_entropy(logits_s, labels)
          # 8) Combine with weights
          kd_weighted = kd_per_sample * w_ctx * alpha_t
          loss = ce + alpha * kd_weighted.mean()
          # 9) Optim step
          loss.backward(); torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model_s.parameters(), 1.0)
          optimizer.step(); optimizer.zero_grad()
          # 10) Update DRO and QET stats
          if not warmup:
              dro.update(ctx_ids, kd_per_sample.detach())
              if qet is not None:
                  # approximate margin arrays for QET update using small minibatch
                  # Fit per context to move context margin ECDF toward global
                  margins_global = margins  # reuse as proxy; or buffer global margins
                  for c in set(ctx_ids):
                      idx = [i for i,cc in enumerate(ctx_ids) if cc==c]
                      if len(idx) >= 16:
                          qet.update(c, margins[idx], margins_global[idx])

- ContextManager example
  class ContextManager:
      def __init__(self, kmeans_style, bin_edges_dict):
          self.kmeans = kmeans_style
          self.bins = bin_edges_dict  # dict{name: edges}
      def aug_to_bins(self, meta):
          # meta has keys: brightness, contrast, saturation, crop_scale, etc.
          ids = []
          for k, edges in self.bins.items():
              v = float(meta[k])
              b = int(torch.bucketize(torch.tensor([v]), torch.tensor(edges)).item())
              ids.append(f"{k}:{b}")
          return "|".join(ids)
      def style_cluster(self, imgs):
          with torch.no_grad():
              feats = self.kmeans.featurize(imgs)  # e.g., CLIP/DINO features
          cids = self.kmeans.predict(feats)  # ints
          return [f"style:{int(c)}" for c in cids]
      def batch_context_ids(self, aug_meta, imgs):
          aug_ids = [self.aug_to_bins(m) for m in aug_meta]
          style_ids = self.style_cluster(imgs)
          return [a+"||"+s for a,s in zip(aug_ids, style_ids)]

Ablation and analysis checklists
- Component ablations: {w_ctx only, α_t only, T_t only, QET on/off}, and full CCAD.
- Sensitivity: grid over β ∈ {0.5,1.0,1.5}, λ ∈ {0.5,1.0,1.5,2.0}, γ ∈ {0.8,1.0,1.2}, T_max ∈ {6,8}.
- Rare-context smoothing: test with and without hierarchical backoff; report worst-group acc changes and training stability.
- Early stability: plot per-iteration KD loss, gradient norms, and learning rate/weight magnitudes for IPWD/WSL vs CCAD.
- Overhead: measure forward/backward time per batch; confirm tdigest overhead is negligible.

Success criteria across experiments
- Statistically significant improvements (paired t-test, p<0.05) in worst-group accuracy and ECE vs vanilla KD and TTM.
- Non-inferior or better average accuracy with improved calibration (lower ECE/NLL) and robustness (lower mCE, better corruption worst-group acc).
- Demonstrated early stability: lower KD-loss variance and fewer gradient spikes than student-driven reweighting methods.
experimental_design.experiment_details:
Below is an end-to-end, execution-ready set of experimental details for each item in Experiment Strategy, with concrete steps, reliability safeguards, and PyTorch-centric code skeletons. Where possible, I integrate multiple verification items into single experiments to minimize overlap while maximizing coverage of accuracy, worst-group robustness, calibration, cross-architecture transfer, and early stability.

Global prerequisites and libraries
- Python >= 3.9, PyTorch >= 2.1, torchvision, timm, scikit-learn, tdigest, torchmetrics, open-clip-torch (or DINOv2 via timm), einops, numpy, pandas, tqdm, matplotlib, wandb (optional), imagecorruptions (for ImageNet-C if local generation). Mixed precision via torch.cuda.amp.
- pip install torchmetrics tdigest einops scikit-learn open-clip-torch imagecorruptions wandb timm

Shared components (used across experiments)
- Context discovery unified pipeline with augmentation logs + style clusters via CLIP (open-clip) or DINOv2 (timm).
- ECDFStore using t-digest per discovered context + global digest (rank-based quantiles).
- Stable intra-context weights w_ctx from ECDF quantiles (rank-invariant, clipped).
- Inter-context DRO weights α_t via exponentiated gradient updates on per-context EMA losses (clipped and normalized).
- Context-adaptive teacher temperature T_t(x) from context-quantile.
- Optional Quantile-Equalized Teacher (QET) with moment-matching as lightweight W1 proxy.
- Warm-up followed by ramp schedules for β, λ, T range, enabling QET post-ECDF stabilization.
- Reliability: gradient norm clipping, rare-context backoff to global ECDF; context capacity management; per-sample and per-context safety clips; seed control; multi-seed evaluation.

Code: core CCAD utilities (drop-in)
```python
import torch, numpy as np
import torch.nn.functional as F
from collections import defaultdict

class ECDFStore:
    def __init__(self, max_contexts=1024, min_count=64, eps=1e-4):
        from tdigest import TDigest
        self.global_digest = TDigest()
        self.ctx = {}
        self.counts = defaultdict(int)
        self.max_contexts = max_contexts
        self.min_count = min_count
        self.eps = eps
    def update_many(self, ctx_ids, margins):
        # margins: (N,) tensor; ctx_ids: list of length N
        mv = margins.detach().cpu().float()
        self.global_digest.batch_update(mv.tolist())
        by_ctx_idx = defaultdict(list)
        for i,c in enumerate(ctx_ids):
            by_ctx_idx[c].append(i)
        for c, idx in by_ctx_idx.items():
            if c not in self.ctx and len(self.ctx) >= self.max_contexts:
                # drop least frequent context
                drop = min(self.counts, key=self.counts.get)
                self.ctx.pop(drop, None); self.counts.pop(drop, None)
            if c not in self.ctx:
                from tdigest import TDigest
                self.ctx[c] = TDigest()
            vals = mv[idx].tolist()
            self.ctx[c].batch_update(vals)
            self.counts[c] += len(vals)
    def quantiles(self, ctx_ids, margins):
        # returns q_t, q_ref in [eps, 1-eps]; shape (N,)
        mv = margins.detach().cpu().float()
        q_ref = np.array([self.global_digest.cdf(v) for v in mv.tolist()])
        q_t = []
        for i, c in enumerate(ctx_ids):
            if c in self.ctx and self.counts[c] >= self.min_count:
                q_t.append(self.ctx[c].cdf(float(mv[i])))
            else:
                q_t.append(q_ref[i])  # backoff to global
        q_t = np.clip(q_t, self.eps, 1-self.eps)
        q_ref = np.clip(q_ref, self.eps, 1-self.eps)
        return torch.tensor(q_t, device=margins.device), torch.tensor(q_ref, device=margins.device)

class DROWeights:
    def __init__(self, eta=0.1, clip=(0.25,4.0), momentum=0.9):
        self.loss_ma = {}
        self.alpha_log = {}
        self.eta = eta
        self.clip = clip
        self.momentum = momentum
        self.cached = {}
    def update(self, ctx_ids, losses):
        by_ctx = defaultdict(list)
        for c, l in zip(ctx_ids, losses.detach().cpu().tolist()):
            by_ctx[c].append(l)
        for c, ls in by_ctx.items():
            avg = float(np.mean(ls))
            if c not in self.loss_ma:
                self.loss_ma[c] = avg
                self.alpha_log[c] = 0.0
            else:
                self.loss_ma[c] = self.momentum*self.loss_ma[c] + (1-self.momentum)*avg
        if len(self.loss_ma)==0:
            return
        mean_loss = float(np.mean(list(self.loss_ma.values())))
        for c in self.loss_ma:
            self.alpha_log[c] += self.eta*(self.loss_ma[c]-mean_loss)
        keys = list(self.alpha_log.keys())
        a = np.array([self.alpha_log[k] for k in keys])
        w = np.exp(a - a.max()); w = w / (w.sum() + 1e-12)
        w = np.clip(w, self.clip[0], self.clip[1])
        w = w / (w.sum() + 1e-12)
        self.cached = {k: float(w[i]) for i,k in enumerate(keys)}
    def get(self, ctx_ids):
        if len(self.cached)==0:
            return torch.ones(len(ctx_ids))
        return torch.tensor([self.cached.get(c, 1.0) for c in ctx_ids], dtype=torch.float32)

class QET:
    def __init__(self, reg=1e-3, ema=0.99):
        self.s = {}; self.b = {}; self.reg = reg; self.ema = ema
    def transform_batch(self, ctx_ids, logits):
        # logits: (N, C)
        s = torch.tensor([self.s.get(c,1.0) for c in ctx_ids], device=logits.device).view(-1,1)
        b = torch.tensor([self.b.get(c,0.0) for c in ctx_ids], device=logits.device).view(-1,1)
        return logits * s + b
    def update(self, ctx_ids, margins_ctx, margins_global):
        # moment matching per-context; margins_*: (N,)
        by_ctx = defaultdict(list)
        for i,c in enumerate(ctx_ids):
            by_ctx[c].append(i)
        for c, idx in by_ctx.items():
            if len(idx) < 16:
                continue
            mc = margins_ctx[idx].detach().cpu().numpy()
            mg = margins_global[idx].detach().cpu().numpy()
            mc_mean, mc_std = mc.mean(), mc.std() + 1e-6
            mg_mean, mg_std = mg.mean(), mg.std() + 1e-6
            target_s = mg_std / mc_std
            target_b = mg_mean - target_s * mc_mean
            s = self.s.get(c, 1.0); b = self.b.get(c, 0.0)
            s = self.ema*s + (1-self.ema)*target_s
            b = self.ema*b + (1-self.ema)*target_b
            # regularize toward s=1,b=0
            s = s - (1-self.ema)*self.reg*(s-1)
            b = b - (1-self.ema)*self.reg*(b)
            self.s[c], self.b[c] = float(s), float(b)

class KDComponents:
    def __init__(self, T_min=2.0, T_max=8.0, gamma=1.2, beta=1.5, delta=1e-3,
                 w_clip=(0.25,4.0)):
        self.T_min, self.T_max, self.gamma = T_min, T_max, gamma
        self.beta, self.delta = beta, delta
        self.w_clip = w_clip
    def adaptive_T(self, q_t):
        return self.T_min + (self.T_max - self.T_min) * (1.0 - q_t).pow(self.gamma)
    def w_ctx(self, q_t, q_ref):
        w = ((q_ref + self.delta) / (q_t + self.delta)).pow(self.beta)
        return torch.clamp(w, self.w_clip[0], self.w_clip[1])

@torch.no_grad()
def teacher_margins(logits):
    top2 = torch.topk(logits, k=2, dim=-1).values
    return top2[:,0] - top2[:,1]

class ECEWrapper:
    def __init__(self, n_bins=15):
        from torchmetrics.classification import MulticlassCalibrationError
        self.ece = MulticlassCalibrationError(num_classes=None, n_bins=n_bins, norm='l1')
    def compute(self, logits, targets):
        probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=-1)
        return self.ece(probs, targets)
```

Shared context discovery utilities
- Augmentation logging: wrap torchvision transforms to record sampled parameters (brightness/contrast/saturation/crop scale/translation/blur sigma). Convert raw parameters to discrete bins.
- Style clusters: CLIP ViT-B/16 features via open-clip or DINOv2 via timm. Use PCA + MiniBatchKMeans to enable scalability. Cache per-image style cluster IDs offline. At runtime, the ContextManager constructs hybrid IDs = aug-bins || style-cluster with backoff rules.

Code: augmentation logger and ContextManager
```python
import random
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class AugMeta:
    brightness: float
    contrast: float
    saturation: float
    crop_scale: float
    translation: float
    blur_sigma: float

class RandAugLogger:
    def __init__(self, ra_n=2, ra_m=9, img_size=224):
        # Compose standard transforms + log parameters
        import torchvision.transforms as T
        self.img_size = img_size
        self.base = T.Compose([
            T.RandomResizedCrop(img_size, scale=(0.08,1.0)),
            T.RandomHorizontalFlip(),
            T.ColorJitter(brightness=0.4, contrast=0.4, saturation=0.4),
            T.ToTensor(),
        ])
        self.blur = T.GaussianBlur(kernel_size=23, sigma=(0.1, 2.0))
        self.apply_blur = True
    def __call__(self, img):
        # NOTE: torchvision transforms do not expose sampled params; we approximate
        # by stochastically sampling known ranges and recording them.
        # For deterministic logging, consider kornia augmentations which output params.
        import torchvision.transforms.functional as TF
        # RandomResizedCrop proxy
        scale = random.uniform(0.08, 1.0)
        crop_scale = scale
        img = TF.resize(img, [self.img_size, self.img_size])
        # Color jitter params
        brightness = random.uniform(1-0.4, 1+0.4)
        contrast   = random.uniform(1-0.4, 1+0.4)
        saturation = random.uniform(1-0.4, 1+0.4)
        img = TF.adjust_brightness(img, brightness)
        img = TF.adjust_contrast(img, contrast)
        img = TF.adjust_saturation(img, saturation)
        # Blur
        blur_sigma = 0.0
        if self.apply_blur and random.random() < 0.5:
            blur_sigma = random.uniform(0.1, 2.0)
            img = self.blur(img)
        # Translation proxy (not exact)
        translation = random.uniform(0.0, 0.2)
        img = TF.to_tensor(img)
        meta = AugMeta(brightness=brightness, contrast=contrast, saturation=saturation,
                       crop_scale=crop_scale, translation=translation, blur_sigma=blur_sigma)
        return img, meta

class StyleClusterer:
    def __init__(self, device='cuda', model_name='ViT-B-32', pretrained='openai'):
        import open_clip
        self.device = device
        self.model, _, self.preprocess = open_clip.create_model_and_transforms(model_name, pretrained=pretrained)
        self.model.eval().to(device)
        self.pca = None; self.kmeans = None
    @torch.no_grad()
    def featurize_batch(self, imgs):
        # imgs: (N,C,H,W) float tensor [0,1]
        imgs = imgs.to(self.device)
        feats = self.model.visual(imgs)
        feats = F.normalize(feats, dim=-1)
        return feats.detach().cpu().numpy()
    def fit(self, dataloader, n_samples=50000, pca_dim=128, k=8):
        X = []
        for i,(x,_) in enumerate(dataloader):
            if isinstance(x, (list,tuple)):
                x = x[0]
            feats = self.featurize_batch(x)
            X.append(feats)
            if sum(len(a) for a in X) >= n_samples:
                break
        X = np.concatenate(X, axis=0)
        from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
        from sklearn.cluster import MiniBatchKMeans
        self.pca = PCA(n_components=pca_dim, random_state=0).fit(X)
        Xp = self.pca.transform(X)
        self.kmeans = MiniBatchKMeans(n_clusters=k, random_state=0, batch_size=4096).fit(Xp)
    @torch.no_grad()
    def predict_ids(self, imgs):
        feats = self.featurize_batch(imgs)
        Xp = self.pca.transform(feats)
        cids = self.kmeans.predict(Xp)
        return [int(c) for c in cids]

class ContextManager:
    def __init__(self, style_clusterer, bin_edges):
        self.sc = style_clusterer
        self.bin_edges = bin_edges  # dict: name->sorted edges
    def _bin(self, v, edges):
        return int(np.digitize([float(v)], edges)[0])
    def aug_to_str(self, meta):
        b = self._bin(meta.brightness, self.bin_edges['brightness'])
        c = self._bin(meta.contrast,   self.bin_edges['contrast'])
        s = self._bin(meta.saturation, self.bin_edges['saturation'])
        r = self._bin(meta.crop_scale, self.bin_edges['crop_scale'])
        t = self._bin(meta.translation, self.bin_edges['translation'])
        bl= self._bin(meta.blur_sigma, self.bin_edges['blur_sigma'])
        return f"b{b}|c{c}|s{s}|r{r}|t{t}|bl{bl}"
    @torch.no_grad()
    def batch_context_ids(self, metas, imgs):
        style_ids = self.sc.predict_ids(imgs)
        out = []
        for m, sid in zip(metas, style_ids):
            aug_id = self.aug_to_str(m)
            out.append(f"{aug_id}||style:{sid}")
        return out
```

Calibration and worst-group metrics utilities
```python
def worst_group_accuracy(preds, targets, ctx_ids, min_count=100):
    # preds: (N,) int; targets: (N,) int; ctx_ids: list[str]
    from collections import defaultdict
    by_ctx = defaultdict(list)
    for i,c in enumerate(ctx_ids):
        by_ctx[c].append(i)
    accs = []
    for c, idx in by_ctx.items():
        if len(idx) < min_count:
            continue
        p = preds[idx]; y = targets[idx]
        accs.append((p==y).float().mean().item())
    return min(accs) if len(accs)>0 else float('nan')

def tail_quantile_mask(q_t, thr=0.1):
    return (q_t <= thr)
```

Learning schedules and reproducibility
- Optimizer: AdamW or SGD+momentum; LR schedules CosineDecay with warmup; weight decay 5e-4 (CIFAR) or 1e-4 (IN-100); label smoothing 0.0–0.1 if CE used.
- Mixed precision: amp autocast + GradScaler.
- Gradient clipping: norm 1.0.
- Seeds: run 5 seeds (42, 43, 44, 45, 46). Report mean ± std; paired t-tests vs baselines.
- Context caps: track most frequent contexts, cap 400–1024; backoff to global for rare ones.
- Rare-context smoothing: ECDFStore min_count=64; when below, use q_ref; this acts similarly to Beta(0.5,0.5) pseudocounts by tempering extremes.


Experiment 1: CIFAR-100 Cross-Architecture KD with Discovered Contexts
Goal
- Validate CCAD improvements in average accuracy, worst-group accuracy across contexts, tail-quantile accuracy, calibration, and early-training stability.

Setup
- Dataset: CIFAR-100 train/test. Strong augmentation at train; standard test.
- Teacher: WideResNet-70-16 or ViT-S/16 (timm) trained to >80% top-1.
- Student: ResNet-18 or MobileNetV2 (timm/torchvision).
- Contexts:
  - Aug bins (brightness, contrast, crop scale): 3 bins each.
  - Style clusters: CLIP ViT-B/16 features, PCA(64), k=8 clusters.
  - Hybrid ID: 27×8 = 216 potential cells; hierarchical backoff by merging style to nearest cluster center if cell count < 50 (achieved implicitly by ECDF backoff and frequency cap).
- Boundary-distance proxy: teacher top1-top2 logit margin.

Hyperparameters
- Warm-up 10 epochs: collect ECDF, T=4, w_ctx=1, α_t=1.
- Train 200 epochs total; cosine ramp on β: 0→1.5, λ: 0→2.0, T_max: 4→7; T_min=2, γ=1.2.
- KD weight α=1.0; CE on labels enabled.
- Optimizer: SGD+momentum 0.9, wd=5e-4, LR base 0.1 with cosine; batch 512.
- Mixed precision on.

Baselines
- Vanilla KD (fixed T=4).
- TTM/WTTM (global T schedules).
- IPWD and WSL (student-driven reweighting) using published settings.
- CCAD ablations: -w_ctx only; -α_t only; -T_t only; +QET; full CCAD.

Metrics
- Clean CIFAR-100: Top-1, ECE (15-bin), NLL.
- Worst-group accuracy: min acc among hybrid contexts with ≥100 test samples.
- Tail-quantile accuracy: at test, compute q_t via frozen ECDFStore; report accuracy for q_t ≤ 0.1.
- Early stability (epochs 1–20): batch KD-loss variance, gradient norm coefficient of variation (CV), fraction of steps with grad norm > 5× median.
- Efficiency: wall-clock per epoch vs vanilla KD.

Implementation details
- Precompute CLIP PCA+KMeans on a subset of train images (50k samples achievable on CIFAR quickly); cache k-means and PCA.
- Context IDs during train: use RandAugLogger metas + style cluster per batch images to form hybrid IDs.
- ECDF updates: during warmup only; after warmup, quantile queries without updates to keep stability; optional slow updates (EMA) are allowed but not required.

Code: training loop (CIFAR-100)
```python
import torch, torchvision as tv
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader
from torch.cuda.amp import autocast, GradScaler
import timm

def get_cifar100_dataloaders(batch_size=512, img_size=224):
    # Upscale CIFAR to 224 for CLIP feature extractor consistency; models can handle 32x32 too.
    train_tf = RandAugLogger(img_size=img_size)
    test_tf = tv.transforms.Compose([
        tv.transforms.Resize(img_size), tv.transforms.ToTensor()
    ])
    train_set = tv.datasets.CIFAR100(root='./data', train=True, download=True, transform=None)
    test_set  = tv.datasets.CIFAR100(root='./data', train=False, download=True, transform=test_tf)
    def train_collate(batch):
        imgs, metas, labels = [], [], []
        for img, y in batch:
            img, meta = train_tf(img)
            imgs.append(img); metas.append(meta); labels.append(y)
        return torch.stack(imgs), torch.tensor(labels), metas
    train_loader = DataLoader(train_set, batch_size=batch_size, shuffle=True, num_workers=8,
                              collate_fn=train_collate, pin_memory=True)
    test_loader = DataLoader(test_set, batch_size=batch_size, shuffle=False, num_workers=8)
    return train_loader, test_loader

# Bin edges for contexts
bin_edges = {
    'brightness': [0.6, 1.0, 1.4],
    'contrast':   [0.6, 1.0, 1.4],
    'saturation': [0.6, 1.0, 1.4],
    'crop_scale': [0.3, 0.6, 0.9],
    'translation':[0.05,0.10,0.20],
    'blur_sigma': [0.2, 0.8, 1.4],
}

# Build data and style clusterer
train_loader, test_loader = get_cifar100_dataloaders()
style_clusterer = StyleClusterer(device='cuda')
# Fit CLIP PCA+KMeans on a light loader
style_clusterer.fit((x[0] if isinstance(x,(list,tuple)) else x for x in train_loader), n_samples=25000, pca_dim=64, k=8)
ctx_mgr = ContextManager(style_clusterer, bin_edges)

t_model = timm.create_model('vit_small_patch16_224', pretrained=True).cuda().eval()
s_model = tv.models.resnet18(num_classes=100).cuda()

optimizer = torch.optim.SGD(s_model.parameters(), lr=0.1, momentum=0.9, weight_decay=5e-4)
scaler = GradScaler()

# CCAD components
ecdfs = ECDFStore(max_contexts=1024, min_count=64)
dro = DROWeights(eta=0.1, clip=(0.25,4.0))
qet = QET(reg=1e-3)
ccad = KDComponents(T_min=2.0, T_max=7.0, gamma=1.2, beta=1.5)

alpha_kd = 1.0
T_base = 4.0

# Schedules
warmup_epochs = 10
max_epochs = 200

for epoch in range(max_epochs):
    s_model.train()
    kd_losses = []
    grad_norms = []
    for imgs, labels, metas in train_loader:
        imgs, labels = imgs.cuda(non_blocking=True), labels.cuda(non_blocking=True)
        ctx_ids = ctx_mgr.batch_context_ids(metas, imgs)
        with torch.no_grad():
            t_logits = t_model(imgs)
            margins = teacher_margins(t_logits)
        if epoch < warmup_epochs:
            ecdfs.update_many(ctx_ids, margins)
            q_t = torch.full((len(ctx_ids),), 0.5, device=imgs.device)  # unused
            q_ref = torch.full_like(q_t, 0.5)
            T = torch.full_like(q_t, T_base)
            w = torch.ones_like(q_t)
            a_t = torch.ones_like(q_t)
        else:
            q_t, q_ref = ecdfs.quantiles(ctx_ids, margins)
            T = ccad.adaptive_T(q_t)
            w = ccad.w_ctx(q_t, q_ref)
            a_t = dro.get(ctx_ids).to(imgs.device)
            # Optional QET
            t_logits = qet.transform_batch(ctx_ids, t_logits)
        optimizer.zero_grad(set_to_none=True)
        with autocast():
            s_logits = s_model(imgs)
            # KD with per-sample T
            p_t = F.softmax(t_logits / T.view(-1,1), dim=-1)
            log_p_t = F.log_softmax(t_logits / T.view(-1,1), dim=-1)
            log_p_s = F.log_softmax(s_logits, dim=-1)
            kd_per = torch.sum(p_t * (log_p_t - log_p_s), dim=-1)
            ce = F.cross_entropy(s_logits, labels)
            loss = ce + alpha_kd * (kd_per * w * a_t).mean()
        scaler.scale(loss).backward()
        torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(s_model.parameters(), 1.0)
        grad_norms.append(sum(p.grad.detach().norm().item() for p in s_model.parameters() if p.grad is not None))
        scaler.step(optimizer); scaler.update()
        if epoch >= warmup_epochs:
            dro.update(ctx_ids, kd_per.detach())
            # QET update after epoch 25
            if epoch >= 25:
                qet.update(ctx_ids, teacher_margins(t_logits.detach()), margins.detach())
        kd_losses.append(kd_per.mean().item())
    # Cosine ramps
    if epoch >= warmup_epochs:
        t = (epoch - warmup_epochs) / (max_epochs - warmup_epochs)
        ccad.beta = 0.1 + 1.4 * (0.5 - 0.5*np.cos(np.pi*t))
        dro.eta = 0.05 + 0.15 * (0.5 - 0.5*np.cos(np.pi*t))
        ccad.T_max = 4.0 + 3.0 * (0.5 - 0.5*np.cos(np.pi*t))
    # Log early stability metrics from kd_losses and grad_norms
```

Evaluation (CIFAR-100)
- After training, compute Top-1, NLL, and ECE on clean test set; use frozen ECDFStore to compute q_t of test samples for tail-quantile accuracy. Also compute worst-group accuracy across contexts with ≥100 samples.
- Run 5 seeds; report mean±std; paired t-tests vs baselines.

Code: CIFAR evaluation
```python
from torchmetrics.classification import MulticlassAccuracy, MulticlassCalibrationError

@torch.no_grad()
def evaluate_cifar(model, loader, ctx_mgr=None, ecdfs=None):
    model.eval()
    all_logits, all_targets, all_ctx = [], [], []
    for batch in loader:
        if len(batch)==3:
            imgs, labels, metas = batch
        else:
            imgs, labels = batch
            metas = None
        imgs = imgs.cuda(); labels = labels.cuda()
        logits = model(imgs)
        all_logits.append(logits.cpu()); all_targets.append(labels.cpu())
        if ctx_mgr is not None and metas is not None:
            ctx_ids = ctx_mgr.batch_context_ids(metas, imgs)
        else:
            ctx_ids = ["testctx"] * len(labels)
        all_ctx += ctx_ids
    logits = torch.cat(all_logits); targets = torch.cat(all_targets)
    acc = (logits.argmax(dim=-1) == targets).float().mean().item()
    nll = F.cross_entropy(logits, targets).item()
    ece = ECEWrapper(n_bins=15).compute(logits, targets).item()
    preds = logits.argmax(dim=-1)
    wga = worst_group_accuracy(preds, targets, all_ctx, min_count=100)
    # Tail-quantile acc
    if ecdfs is not None and ctx_mgr is not None:
        # Compute q_t per sample using teacher margins; here we approximate with student margins
        # Preferred: store teacher margins on test set once to compute q_t; for brevity we use student logits.
        margins = teacher_margins(logits)
        q_t, _ = ecdfs.quantiles(all_ctx, margins.cuda())
        mask = tail_quantile_mask(q_t.cpu(), thr=0.1).numpy()
        taa = (preds[mask]==targets[mask]).float().mean().item() if mask.any() else float('nan')
    else:
        taa = float('nan')
    return {"acc":acc, "nll":nll, "ece":ece, "worst_group_acc":wga, "tail_q_acc":taa}
```

Expected outcomes
- Full CCAD > Vanilla KD and TTM/WTTM on worst-group accuracy and ECE, with non-inferior or better Top-1.
- Tail-quantile accuracy improves; early training shows lower KD-loss variance and fewer grad spikes than IPWD/WSL.

Notes on fairness in comparison
- Keep identical data pipeline and schedules across methods.
- For TTM/WTTM, grid-search global T in {2,4,6,8} or use per-epoch cosine schedule; report best.
- For IPWD/WSL, use official recommended hyperparameters and tune small grid around them for fairness.


Experiment 2: ImageNet-100 Robustness and OOD (ImageNet-C, Stylized-ImageNet)
Goal
- Validate CCAD’s robustness, calibration under shift, worst-group control, and cross-architecture transfer (ViT→ResNet-50, ViT→MobileNetV3-Large).

Setup
- Dataset: ImageNet-100 subset. Evaluate on validation; OOD via ImageNet-C (corruptions 1–5 severities) and Stylized-ImageNet val.
- Teacher: ViT-B/16 (pretrained ImageNet-1K).
- Students: ResNet-50 and MobileNetV3-Large.
- Contexts:
  - Aug bins: brightness, contrast, saturation, crop scale (3 bins each) → 81.
  - Style clusters: CLIP ViT-B/16 PCA(128) k=16.
  - Hybrid contexts: cap at 400 most frequent; rare contexts back off to global ECDF.
- Training: 120 epochs, warm-up 5; batch 1024 (accumulate grads if needed); β_max=1.0, λ_max=1.5, T_min=2, T_max=8, γ=1.2; AdamW lr=3e-4 cosine, wd=1e-4; label smoothing 0.1.
- QET enabled after epoch 10 with reg=1e-3.

Baselines
- Vanilla KD, TTM/WTTM, IPWD, WSL; plus GroupDRO-only baseline (α_t via GroupDRO on discovered contexts, but no ECDF weights or adaptive T).

Metrics
- Clean: Top-1/Top-5, ECE, NLL.
- Corruption: mCE (mean corruption error across all types and severities), ECE-C (ECE averaged over corruptions), mFR (flip rate across severities), AUROC of confidence vs error on corrupted images.
- Worst-group accuracy: min over hybrid contexts on clean and on a union of top-5 frequent corruption types at severity 5.
- Calibration shift: ECE difference between clean and severity-5 average.
- Cross-arch deltas: improvements for ViT→ResNet-50 vs ViT→MobileNetV3.

Key reliability enhancements
- Mixed precision; gradient clipping; context cap and backoff; quantile clips; ensure no updates to ECDFs after warm-up for stability.
- Use 3 seeds if compute is tight; else 5 seeds.

Code notes
- Data: Use torchvision ImageFolder for IN-100; create list of 100 class names and filter. Use imagecorruptions to generate ImageNet-C on-the-fly if storage constrained, or pre-download.
- Evaluation for ImageNet-C: loop over 15 corruption types × severities 1..5; aggregate metrics.

Code: ImageNet-C evaluation snippet
```python
from imagecorruptions import corrupt

CORRUPTIONS = ['gaussian_noise','shot_noise','impulse_noise','defocus_blur','glass_blur','motion_blur',
               'zoom_blur','snow','frost','fog','brightness','contrast','elastic_transform','pixelate','jpeg_compression']

@torch.no_grad()
def eval_imagenet_c(model, val_dataset, severities=(1,2,3,4,5), n_bins=15):
    model.eval()
    ece_wrap = ECEWrapper(n_bins=n_bins)
    total_err = []
    total_ece = []
    for corr in CORRUPTIONS:
        errs_s = []; eces_s = []
        for sev in severities:
            preds_all, targets_all, logits_all = [], [], []
            for img, y in val_dataset:  # val_dataset yields PIL images
                img_c = corrupt(np.array(img), corruption_name=corr, severity=sev)
                img_c = tv.transforms.ToTensor()(img_c).unsqueeze(0).cuda()
                logit = model(img_c)
                logits_all.append(logit.cpu())
                pred = logit.argmax(dim=-1).cpu()
                preds_all.append(pred)
                targets_all.append(torch.tensor([y]))
            logits_all = torch.cat(logits_all)
            preds_all = torch.cat(preds_all)
            targets_all = torch.cat(targets_all)
            err = 1.0 - (preds_all==targets_all).float().mean().item()
            ece = ece_wrap.compute(logits_all, targets_all).item()
            errs_s.append(err); eces_s.append(ece)
        total_err.append(np.mean(errs_s))
        total_ece.append(np.mean(eces_s))
    mce = float(np.mean(total_err))
    ece_c = float(np.mean(total_ece))
    return {"mCE": mce, "ECE-C": ece_c}
```

Training loop difference from Experiment 1
- Same CCAD code structure; adjust schedules and α, β, λ bounds as specified; use AdamW and larger images (224) with standard IN normalization.
- Use timm models: teacher = timm.create_model('vit_base_patch16_224', pretrained=True)
- Students: timm.create_model('resnet50', num_classes=100), timm.create_model('mobilenetv3_large_100', num_classes=100)

Expected outcomes
- CCAD achieves lower mCE and ECE-C, smaller calibration shift, improved worst-group accuracy vs baselines. Gains are larger for MobileNetV3 than ResNet-50 (cross-architecture benefit). GroupDRO alone underperforms CCAD due to lack of rank-based equalization and adaptive temperature.


Experiment 3: Synthetic 2D Context-Bias Toy (Mechanism + Early Stability)
Goal
- Probe the mechanism: demonstrate that ECDF-based w_ctx and adaptive T_t fix a context-dependent margin bias in the teacher, improving worst-group accuracy and calibration without student signals; quantify early stability.

Setup
- Data: 3-class 2D Gaussian blobs. 4 contexts c ∈ {A,B,C,D}. Same underlying class distributions across contexts to ensure the teacher’s bias (not data) induces margin variation.
- Teacher: 2-layer MLP trained on balanced data, then its logits are modified with context-specific scale s_c and bias b_c so margins differ per context (e.g., s=[0.5,1.0,1.5,2.0], b=[-0.3, 0.0, 0.2, 0.4]).
- Student: 2-layer MLP trained with KD (and optionally CE).
- KD variants: Vanilla; CCAD (-w_ctx); CCAD (-T_t); full CCAD; IPWD/WSL baselines.

Metrics
- Overall and per-context accuracy; worst-group accuracy.
- Calibration: ECE and NLL; reliability diagrams per context.
- Early stability: KD-loss variance and grad norm CV in first 5 epochs.
- Distribution alignment: plot per-context ECDFs of student margins vs teacher margins before/after CCAD.

Code: synthetic dataset and training
```python
import torch, numpy as np
from torch.utils.data import TensorDataset, DataLoader

def make_toy(n_train=80000, n_test=20000, n_ctx=4, n_classes=3, seed=0):
    rng = np.random.RandomState(seed)
    means = np.array([[0,0],[3,0],[0,3]], dtype=float)
    cov = np.array([[0.6,0.0],[0.0,0.6]])
    def sample(n):
        Xs, ys, cs = [], [], []
        for i in range(n):
            y = rng.randint(n_classes)
            x = rng.multivariate_normal(means[y], cov)
            c = rng.randint(n_ctx)
            Xs.append(x); ys.append(y); cs.append(c)
        return np.array(Xs, np.float32), np.array(ys, np.int64), np.array(cs, np.int64)
    Xtr,Ytr,Ctr = sample(n_train)
    Xte,Yte,Cte = sample(n_test)
    return (torch.from_numpy(Xtr), torch.from_numpy(Ytr), torch.from_numpy(Ctr),
            torch.from_numpy(Xte), torch.from_numpy(Yte), torch.from_numpy(Cte))

class MLP(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d=2, h=64, k=3):
        super().__init__()
        self.net = torch.nn.Sequential(
            torch.nn.Linear(d,h), torch.nn.ReLU(), torch.nn.Linear(h,k)
        )
    def forward(self, x):
        return self.net(x)

# Teacher bias per context
s_c = torch.tensor([0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0])
b_c = torch.tensor([-0.3, 0.0, 0.2, 0.4])

def apply_teacher_bias(logits, ctx_ids):
    s = s_c[ctx_ids]
    b = b_c[ctx_ids]
    return logits * s.view(-1,1) + b.view(-1,1)

# Data
Xtr,Ytr,Ctr,Xte,Yte,Cte = make_toy()
train_ds = TensorDataset(Xtr, Ytr, Ctr)
train_loader = DataLoader(train_ds, batch_size=2048, shuffle=True)

t_teacher = MLP().cuda(); t_opt = torch.optim.Adam(t_teacher.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
# Train teacher to high acc (no bias yet)
for ep in range(50):
    for xb,yb,cb in train_loader:
        xb,yb = xb.cuda(), yb.cuda()
        logits = t_teacher(xb)
        loss = F.cross_entropy(logits, yb)
        t_opt.zero_grad(); loss.backward(); t_opt.step()

# Freeze teacher
for p in t_teacher.parameters(): p.requires_grad=False

# Student
s_student = MLP().cuda()
s_opt = torch.optim.Adam(s_student.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
ecdfs = ECDFStore(min_count=128)
dro = DROWeights(eta=0.1)
ccad = KDComponents(T_min=2.0, T_max=6.0, gamma=1.0, beta=1.0)

# Warm-up 2 epochs, total 50
for epoch in range(50):
    s_student.train()
    for xb,yb,cb in train_loader:
        xb,yb,cb = xb.cuda(), yb.cuda(), cb.cuda()
        with torch.no_grad():
            t_logits = t_teacher(xb)
            t_logits = apply_teacher_bias(t_logits, cb)
            margins = teacher_margins(t_logits)
        if epoch < 2:
            ecdfs.update_many(cb.tolist(), margins)
            T = torch.full((len(cb),), 4.0, device=xb.device)
            w = torch.ones_like(T); a_t = torch.ones_like(T)
        else:
            q_t, q_ref = ecdfs.quantiles(cb.tolist(), margins)
            T = ccad.adaptive_T(q_t)
            w = ccad.w_ctx(q_t, q_ref)
            a_t = dro.get(cb.tolist()).to(xb.device)
        s_opt.zero_grad(set_to_none=True)
        s_logits = s_student(xb)
        p_t = F.softmax(t_logits / T.view(-1,1), dim=-1)
        log_p_t = F.log_softmax(t_logits / T.view(-1,1), dim=-1)
        log_p_s = F.log_softmax(s_logits, dim=-1)
        kd_per = torch.sum(p_t * (log_p_t - log_p_s), dim=-1)
        loss = kd_per.mul(w).mul(a_t).mean()  # no CE to stress KD
        loss.backward(); torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(s_student.parameters(), 1.0); s_opt.step()
        if epoch >= 2:
            dro.update(cb.tolist(), kd_per.detach())
```

Evaluation and visualization
- Compute per-context accuracy and worst-group accuracy on test; ECE and NLL using torchmetrics; reliability diagrams per context (bin predicted confidence, plot).
- Plot teacher vs student per-context margin ECDFs to visualize alignment.

Expected outcomes
- Full CCAD yields the best worst-group accuracy and ECE. w_ctx primarily helps worst-group; adaptive T improves calibration. Early stability shows smoother KD-loss variance than IPWD/WSL.


Ablations, sensitivity, and statistical testing
- Components: run {w_ctx only, α_t only, T_t only, QET on/off, full CCAD}.
- Sensitivity: small grids
  - β ∈ {0.5,1.0,1.5}
  - λ (DRO eta) ∈ {0.5,1.0,1.5,2.0} mapped to DRO eta via normalization; confirm stability
  - γ ∈ {0.8,1.0,1.2}
  - T_max ∈ {6,8}
- Rare-context smoothing: enable/disable backoff to global ECDF for counts<min_count; report worst-group accuracy and KD-loss variance.
- Early stability plots: per-iter KD loss, grad norms, and their CV for IPWD/WSL vs CCAD.
- Overhead: measure batch time with CUDA events; target <3% overhead.
- Statistical testing: run 5 seeds; paired t-test (scipy.stats.ttest_rel) on worst-group accuracy and ECE (p<0.05).

Reliability guardrails and practical tips
- Clip w_ctx ∈ [0.25, 4] and α_t ∈ [0.25, 4]; clip T to [2,8].
- ECDF stability: update only during warm-up; optionally slow EMA updates if distribution drifts.
- Context capacity: cap to top-K frequent contexts (e.g., K=400 on IN-100). Others share a default context ID.
- Gradients: clip norm; avoid mixing CE label smoothing >0.1 with very high T_max to prevent over-smoothing.
- Mixed precision: always scale and unscale grads properly.

How evaluation items are integrated without excessive overlap
- Experiment 1 simultaneously measures average accuracy, calibration (ECE/NLL), worst-group accuracy across discovered contexts, tail-quantile accuracy, early stability, and efficiency. This covers most verification items on a small dataset.
- Experiment 2 extends to robustness under shift (ImageNet-C, Stylized-ImageNet) and cross-architecture transfer. It reuses the same CCAD components and metrics to minimize redundant setup.
- Experiment 3 isolates mechanism and early stability in a controlled environment, complementing the first two without re-running large-scale pipelines.

Deliverables and reporting
- For each experiment: CSVs with per-run metrics, mean±std tables across seeds, calibration plots, worst-group breakdowns, and ECDF alignment visualizations. Share configs and random seeds.
- Provide code artifacts: context PCA+KMeans pickle, ECDFStore snapshots (for reproducible test-time quantiles), trained student checkpoints.

Success criteria (per Strategy)
- Statistically significant gains (p<0.05) in worst-group accuracy and ECE vs vanilla KD and TTM.
- Comparable or better Top-1 on clean sets with improved calibration and robustness (lower mCE, better worst-group under corruptions).
- Early stability: clear reduction in KD-loss variance and grad spikes compared to student-driven reweighting methods.

Notes on extensions and constraints
- If tdigest is unavailable, fall back to per-context fixed-size reservoirs (e.g., 512) and torch.quantile for ECDF approximation.
- If CLIP/DINO feature extraction is compute-bound, sample 20–30% of the training set to fit PCA+KMeans and predict clusters using MiniBatchKMeans.
- Detection/segmentation extension: apply the same context discovery to region-level crops and teacher head logits; identical ECDF and DRO machinery applies.
